note: Twenty-four papers behind the methods and the story. Every DOI was checked against the publisher of record. Hooks are written for this atlas, not lifted from abstracts.

papers.authors: Daniel Aaronson, Daniel Hartley & Bhashkar Mazumder
papers.title: The Effects of the 1930s HOLC “Redlining” Maps
papers.hook: The closest thing to a causal estimate of what the HOLC maps did: tracts that fell just on the red side of a boundary diverged for decades. The backbone of this atlas's redlining module.

papers.title: We Built This: Consequences of New Deal Era Intervention in America's Racial Geography
papers.hook: Shows the maps did not just record segregation, they manufactured it, by writing “proximity to Black residents lowers value” into federal underwriting.

papers.authors: Jeremy S. Hoffman, Vivek Shandas & Nicholas Pendleton
papers.title: The Effects of Historical Housing Policies on Resident Exposure to Intra-Urban Heat: A Study of 108 US Urban Areas
papers.hook: Formerly redlined neighborhoods run hotter today, by up to 7°C. A vivid case that a 1939 ink color still shapes the physical environment.

papers.title: Structural Racism, Historical Redlining, and Risk of Preterm Birth in New York City, 2013–2017
papers.hook: Preterm-birth risk climbs monotonically from grade A to grade D. The health gradient this atlas measures with diabetes, replicated at birth.

papers.authors: Douglas S. Massey & Nancy A. Denton
papers.title: The Dimensions of Residential Segregation
papers.hook: Names the five axes of segregation (evenness, exposure, concentration, centralization, clustering). Why one dissimilarity index is never the whole picture.

papers.authors: David M. Cutler & Edward L. Glaeser
papers.title: Are Ghettos Good or Bad?
papers.hook: Uses city-to-city variation to ask whether segregation itself harms outcomes. The template for treating a map as data, not decoration.

papers.authors: David M. Cutler, Edward L. Glaeser & Jacob L. Vigdor
papers.title: The Rise and Decline of the American Ghetto
papers.hook: A century of segregation indices, 1890–1990, and the institutions that drove them up and then partly down.

papers.title: Housing Segregation, Negro Employment, and Metropolitan Decentralization
papers.hook: The original spatial-mismatch argument: separate where people live from where the jobs are and unemployment follows. Still the frame for South Central.

papers.title: The Wrong Side(s) of the Tracks: The Causal Effects of Racial Segregation on Urban Poverty and Inequality
papers.hook: Instruments segregation with the literal layout of 19th-century railroad tracks. A clean lesson in finding exogenous variation in a map.

papers.title: Was Postwar Suburbanization “White Flight”? Evidence from the Black Migration
papers.hook: Quantifies how many white residents left cities per Black arrival. Movement, not just stocks, makes the modern map.

papers.title: Income Inequality and Income Segregation
papers.hook: Rising inequality sorts families across neighborhoods, mostly by concentrating affluence. Connects the income choropleth to the segregation one.

papers.authors: David Card, Alexandre Mas & Jesse Rothstein
papers.title: Tipping and the Dynamics of Segregation
papers.hook: Finds discontinuities: once minority share crosses a tract-specific threshold, white population collapses. A regression-discontinuity classic on a map.

papers.authors: Raj Chetty, Nathaniel Hendren, Patrick Kline & Emmanuel Saez
papers.title: Where Is the Land of Opportunity? The Geography of Intergenerational Mobility in the United States
papers.hook: Mobility is local and the rank-rank relationship is strikingly linear, exactly the conditional-expectation idea this atlas teaches in M3.

papers.title: The Impacts of Neighborhoods on Intergenerational Mobility I: Childhood Exposure Effects
papers.hook: Each childhood year in a better tract pays off linearly. Turns “place matters” into a dose-response curve.

papers.authors: Raj Chetty, Nathaniel Hendren & Lawrence F. Katz
papers.title: The Effects of Exposure to Better Neighborhoods on Children: New Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment
papers.hook: The randomized-voucher experiment that gives the neighborhood story a true counterfactual.

papers.title: Notes on Continuous Stochastic Phenomena
papers.hook: The origin of Moran's I, the statistic this atlas uses to prove that nearby tracts resemble each other more than chance allows.

papers.title: Local Indicators of Spatial Association—LISA
papers.hook: Breaks a global Moran's I into per-tract contributions, the math behind hot-spot and cold-spot maps.

papers.title: A Computer Movie Simulating Urban Growth in the Detroit Region
papers.hook: Source of the First Law of Geography: everything relates to everything, but near things more so. The one sentence every spatial model assumes.

papers.title: Increasing Returns and Economic Geography
papers.hook: Why economic activity clumps into cores and peripheries at all. The theory under the density choropleth.

papers.authors: Robert J. Sampson, Stephen W. Raudenbush & Felton Earls
papers.title: Neighborhoods and Violent Crime: A Multilevel Study of Collective Efficacy
papers.hook: Introduces collective efficacy and the multilevel model, the reason we cannot treat tracts as independent observations.

papers.title: Rising Morbidity and Mortality in Midlife among White Non-Hispanic Americans in the 21st Century
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 112(49): 15078–15083
papers.hook: The “deaths of despair” paper. A reminder that health gradients move, and that descriptive statistics can be an alarm.

papers.title: The Association Between Income and Life Expectancy in the United States, 2001–2014
papers.hook: Life expectancy tracks income, and the gap varies by place. The clearest national version of the within-California health map.

papers.authors: Rebecca Diamond, Tim McQuade & Franklin Qian
papers.title: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evidence from San Francisco
papers.hook: A California-grown quasi-experiment: rent control helped incumbents stay but shrank supply. Policy impact estimated cleanly, the M7 goal.

papers.authors: Trevon D. Logan & John M. Parman
papers.title: The National Rise in Residential Segregation
papers.hook: Builds a next-door-neighbor segregation measure from full census manuscripts back to 1880. Shows segregation is a 20th-century construction, not a constant.
